sentences.chinese: 蘇林曰：「『言之不從，是謂不艾，則金不從革。』
sentences.literal: Su Lin said: "'If words are not heeded, that is called not governing—then metal will not obey smelting.'"
sentences.idiomatic: Su Lin cites the Hong fan: disobedience in counsel skews the metal phase.

sentences.chinese: 景，象也。
sentences.literal: Jing means image.
sentences.idiomatic: Jing means the portent or shadow cast.

sentences.chinese: 何象，言將危亡也。」
sentences.literal: What image? It speaks of imminent peril and ruin."
sentences.idiomatic: The shadow foretells collapse."

sentences.chinese: 〉漢興以來，社稷二危。
sentences.literal: 〉Since Han rose, the altars of soil and grain have twice stood in peril.
sentences.idiomatic: Since Gaozu the dynastic altars have twice tottered.

sentences.chinese: 呂、霍、上官皆母后之家也，親親之道，全之爲右，〈師古曰：「務全安之，此爲上。」
sentences.literal: The Lu, Huo, and Shangguan houses were all the empress dowagers' kin; in the way of loving kin, to keep them whole is best—〈Yan's note: "Strive to keep them secure—this is highest."〉
sentences.idiomatic: The Lu, Huo, and Shangguan clans were maternal relatives; kin should be sheltered—Yan Shigu says keeping them safe ranks first.

sentences.chinese: 〉當與之賢師良傅，敎以忠孝之道。
sentences.literal: 〉One should give them worthy teachers and good tutors, instructing them in the way of loyalty and filial piety.
sentences.idiomatic: Give them wise tutors and drill loyalty and filial piety.

sentences.chinese: 今迺尊寵其位，授以魁柄，〈師古曰：「以斗爲喻也，斗身爲魁。」
sentences.literal: Now you instead honor and dote on their stations and hand them the dipper's handle—〈Yan's note: "Using the dipper as metaphor—the dipper's body is the kui."〉
sentences.idiomatic: Instead you fatten their offices and hand them the Big Dipper's handle—Yan Shigu compares power to the ladle stars.

sentences.chinese: 〉使之驕逆，至於夷滅，〈師古曰：「夷，平也，謂平除之。」
sentences.literal: 〉letting them grow arrogant and rebellious until they are leveled and exterminated—〈Yan's note: "Yi means level—utterly cleared."〉
sentences.idiomatic: Until they turn arrogant and are wiped root and branch—Yan Shigu glosses yi as level away.

sentences.chinese: 〉此失親親之大者也。
sentences.literal: 〉this is the great failure in loving kin.
sentences.idiomatic: That is how loving kin goes wrong.

sentences.chinese: 自霍光之賢，不能爲子孫慮，故權臣易世則危。
sentences.literal: Even Huo Guang's worth could not plan for sons and grandsons—therefore when power ministers change reigns, danger follows.
sentences.idiomatic: Even Huo Guang could not save his line—so when regents survive a change of throne, peril follows.

sentences.chinese: 書曰：「毋若火，始庸庸。」
sentences.literal: The Document says, "Do not be like fire, which at first smolders."
sentences.idiomatic: The Classic warns: do not let power smolder like kindling fire.

sentences.chinese: 〈師古曰：「周書洛誥之辭也。
sentences.literal: 〈Yan's note: "It is a saying in the Zhou document 'Luo gao.'"
sentences.idiomatic: Yan Shigu: from the Luo gao in the Zhou Documents.

sentences.chinese: 庸庸，微小貌也。
sentences.literal: Yong yong means the appearance of tiny.
sentences.idiomatic: Yong yong describes a tiny spark.

sentences.chinese: 言火始微小，不早撲滅則至熾盛。
sentences.literal: It means fire at first is small; if you do not stamp it out early it reaches blazing strength.
sentences.idiomatic: A spark ignored becomes an inferno.

sentences.chinese: 大臣貴擅，亦當早圖黜其權也。」
sentences.literal: When great ministers grow noble and overbearing, you should early plan to strip their power."
sentences.idiomatic: When ministers swell, cut their power early."

sentences.chinese: 〉埶陵於君，權隆於主，然後防之，亦亡及已。
sentences.literal: 〉When their power overtops the ruler and their authority looms above the sovereign, then you try to fence them—it is also too late.
sentences.idiomatic: Wait until they dwarf the throne and it is too late to fence them in.

sentences.chinese: 〈師古曰：「已，語終辭。」〉
sentences.literal: 〈Yan's note: "Yi is a closing particle."〉
sentences.idiomatic: Yan Shigu: yi closes the sentence.

sentences.chinese: 上遂不納。
sentences.literal: Thereupon the emperor did not accept.
sentences.idiomatic: The throne ignored the memorial.

sentences.chinese: 成帝乆亡繼嗣，福以爲宜建三統，封孔子之世以爲殷後，復上書曰：
sentences.literal: Emperor Cheng long lacked an heir; Fu thought it fit to establish the three lineages, enfeoffing Confucius's descendants as Yin successors, and again submitted a memorial saying:
sentences.idiomatic: Cheng still had no heir; Mei Fu urged reviving the three royal lines by enfeoffing Confucius's house as heir to Yin, and wrote again:

sentences.chinese: 臣聞「不在其位，不謀其政」。
sentences.literal: Your servant has heard, "Not in that office, do not seek that government."
sentences.idiomatic: The classic says: out of office, out of policy.

sentences.chinese: 政者職也，位卑而言高者罪也。
sentences.literal: Government is duty; to speak high from a low station is a crime.
sentences.idiomatic: Policy belongs to rank; humble men who lecture upward commit a crime.

sentences.chinese: 越職觸罪，危言世患，雖伏質橫分，臣之願也。
sentences.literal: To overstep duty and touch crime, to speak dangerously of the age's ills—though I lie split on the block, it is your servant's wish.
sentences.idiomatic: I risk the waist block to name this age's sickness—gladly.

sentences.chinese: 〈師古曰：「伏質，斬刑也。
sentences.literal: 〈Yan's note: "Fu zhi is the beheading stroke."
sentences.idiomatic: Yan Shigu: fu zhi is execution.

sentences.chinese: 橫分，謂身首分離也。」
sentences.literal: Heng fen means body and head parted."〉
sentences.idiomatic: Heng fen means cut in two."

sentences.chinese: 〉守職不言，沒齒身全，死之日，尸未腐而名滅，雖有景公之位，伏歷千駟，臣不貪也。
sentences.literal: 〉To keep office and not speak, to die with teeth intact—on the day of death, before the corpse rots the name is gone; though you had Duke Jing's station and a thousand four-horse teams beneath the shaft, your servant would not covet it.
sentences.idiomatic: Silent clerks die whole yet nameless before the flesh cools; I would not trade this for Qi Jing's thousand teams.

sentences.chinese: 〈師古曰：「景公，齊景公也。
sentences.literal: 〈Yan's note: "Duke Jing is Duke Jing of Qi."
sentences.idiomatic: Yan Shigu identifies Qi Jinggong.

sentences.chinese: 論語云：『齊景公有馬千駟，死之日，民無得而稱焉。』
sentences.literal: The Analects says, "Duke Jing of Qi had a thousand teams of four horses, yet the day he died the people found nothing to praise him for."
sentences.idiomatic: The Analects says Jing's thousand teams won him no praise after death.

sentences.chinese: 故引之也。」
sentences.literal: Therefore it is cited."〉
sentences.idiomatic: Hence the quotation."

sentences.chinese: 〉故願壹登文石之陛，涉赤墀之塗，〈應劭曰：「以丹淹泥塗殿上也。」
sentences.literal: 〉Therefore I wish once to ascend the patterned stone steps and tread the crimson courtyard's mud—〈Ying Shao's note: "They smeared the hall with cinnabar mud."〉
sentences.idiomatic: I beg once to climb the marble stairs and the cinnabar court—Ying Shao notes the red-daubed floor.

sentences.chinese: 〉當戶牖之法坐，〈師古曰：「戶牖之間謂之扆，言負扆也。
sentences.literal: 〉to sit at the lawful seat between the doors—〈Yan's note: "The space between doors is called yi—meaning you lean on the yi screen.
sentences.idiomatic: To sit at the proper throne between the pillars—Yan Shigu names the yi screen behind the ruler.

sentences.chinese: 法坐，正坐也，聽朝之處，猶言法官、法駕也。
sentences.literal: Fa zuo means the correct seat, the place of hearing court—like saying fa guan, fa jia."
sentences.idiomatic: Fa zuo is the formal audience seat, like "law carriage."

sentences.chinese: 坐音才卧反。」
sentences.literal: Zuo is read cai-wo fan."〉
sentences.idiomatic: Fanqie for zuo."

sentences.chinese: 〉盡平生之愚慮。
sentences.literal: 〉and exhaust your servant's foolish thoughts.
sentences.idiomatic: and pour out every thought I have.

sentences.chinese: 亡益於時，有遺於世，〈師古曰：「遺，留也。」
sentences.literal: If it brings no benefit to the times yet leaves something for posterity—〈Yan's note: "Yi means to leave behind."〉
sentences.idiomatic: If it helps the age not at all yet leaves a trace—Yan Shigu: yi is leave behind.

sentences.chinese: 〉此臣寢所以不安，食所以忘味也。
sentences.literal: 〉that is why your servant's sleep is not peaceful and his food loses taste.
sentences.idiomatic: That is why I cannot sleep or taste my food.

sentences.chinese: 願陛下深省臣言。
sentences.literal: I beg Your Majesty deeply examine your servant's words.
sentences.idiomatic: I beg you weigh every line.

sentences.chinese: 〈師古曰：「省，察也。」〉
sentences.literal: 〈Yan's note: "Xing means to examine."〉
sentences.idiomatic: Yan Shigu: xing is examine.

sentences.chinese: 臣聞存人所以自立也，壅人所以自塞也。
sentences.literal: Your servant has heard that preserving others is the means to establish oneself, and blocking others is the means to block oneself.
sentences.idiomatic: Help others rise and you rise; choke them and you choke yourself.

sentences.chinese: 善惡之報，各如其事。
sentences.literal: The requital of good and evil matches each deed.
sentences.idiomatic: Good and evil reap their own fruit.

sentences.chinese: 昔者秦滅二周，夷六國，〈師古曰：「二周，東周、西周君也。
sentences.literal: Formerly Qin destroyed the two Zhous and leveled the six states—〈Yan's note: "The two Zhous are the lords of Eastern and Western Zhou.
sentences.idiomatic: Qin snuffed Eastern and Western Zhou and six kingdoms—Yan Shigu names the Zhou royal houses.

sentences.chinese: 六國，齊、楚、韓、魏、趙、燕。」
sentences.literal: The six states are Qi, Chu, Han, Wei, Zhao, Yan."〉
sentences.idiomatic: The six are Qi, Chu, Han, Wei, Zhao, Yan."

sentences.chinese: 〉隱士不顯，佚民不舉，〈師古曰：「佚與逸同。」
sentences.literal: 〉recluses were not displayed, wandering men were not raised—〈Yan's note: "Yi is the same as yi 'ease' in the sense of reclusion."〉
sentences.idiomatic: Hermits went unhonored, wanderers uncalled—Yan Shigu reads yi as reclusion.

sentences.chinese: 〉絕三統，滅天道，是以身危子殺，厥孫不嗣，〈張晏曰：「身爲燕丹、張良所謀，子二世見殺。
sentences.literal: 〉cut off the three lineages and extinguished Heaven's way—therefore his person was endangered, his son killed, his grandson could not succeed—〈Zhang Yan's note: "His person was plotted against by Jing Dan and Zhang Liang; his son the Second Generation was killed.
sentences.idiomatic: Qin severed the three royal lines and snuffed Heaven's pattern—so the First Emperor fell to plots, Ershi died, Ying could not inherit—Zhang Yan glosses the line.

sentences.chinese: 孫謂子嬰。」
sentences.literal: The grandson means Ziying."〉
sentences.idiomatic: The grandson is Ziying."

sentences.chinese: 〉所謂壅人以自塞者也。
sentences.literal: 〉this is what is called blocking others and thereby blocking oneself.
sentences.idiomatic: That is choking others and choking yourself.

sentences.chinese: 故武王克殷，未下車，存五帝之後，封殷於宋，紹夏於杞，〈師古曰：「謂封黃帝之後於薊，帝堯之後於祝，帝舜之後於陳，并杞、宋，是爲五帝。」
sentences.literal: Therefore when King Wu conquered Yin, before he stepped down from the chariot he preserved the heirs of the Five Thearchs, enfeoffed Yin in Song and continued Xia in Qi—〈Yan's note: "He means enfeoffing Huang Di's heir at Ji, Yao's at Zhu, Shun's at Chen, together with Qi and Song—making five."〉
sentences.idiomatic: King Wu, still in his chariot, restored the five royal lines—Song for Yin, Qi for Xia—Yan Shigu lists the five enfeoffments.

sentences.chinese: 〉明著三統，示不獨有也。
sentences.literal: 〉thereby clarifying the three lineages and showing he did not monopolize.
sentences.idiomatic: He showed the three lines of succession so no one house owned Heaven.

sentences.chinese: 是以姬姓半天下，遷廟之主，流出於戶，〈李竒曰：「言其多。」
sentences.literal: Therefore the Ji surname held half the realm; wandering ancestral tablets flowed out the doors—〈Li Qi's note: "It means they were very many."〉
sentences.idiomatic: So half the realm bore the Ji name and portable shrines crowded every gate—Li Qi stresses their number.

sentences.chinese: 〉所謂存人以自立者也。
sentences.literal: 〉this is what is called preserving others to establish oneself.
sentences.idiomatic: That is helping others to stand firm yourself.

sentences.chinese: 今成湯不祀，殷人亡後。
sentences.literal: Now Cheng Tang receives no sacrifice and Yin has no successor.
sentences.idiomatic: King Tang's line lacks smoke on the altar; Yin has no heir.

sentences.chinese: 陛下繼嗣乆微，殆爲此也。
sentences.literal: Your Majesty's heir has long been scarce—perhaps it is because of this.
sentences.idiomatic: Your own heir-lessness may stem from the same neglect.

sentences.chinese: 春秋經曰：「宋殺其大夫。」
sentences.literal: The Spring and Autumn Annals records, "Song put its great officer to death."
sentences.idiomatic: The Annals record Song executing a great officer.

sentences.chinese: 穀梁傳曰：「其不稱名姓，以其在祖位，尊之也。」
sentences.literal: The Guliang commentary says, "It does not give name or surname because he stood in the ancestor's place—therefore he is honored."
sentences.idiomatic: Guliang says the victim went unnamed because he sat in the ancestral seat—honored silence.

sentences.chinese: 〈師古曰：「事在僖二十五年。
sentences.literal: 〈Yan's note: "The affair is in Xi 25.
sentences.idiomatic: Yan Shigu: the entry falls in Xi 25.

sentences.chinese: 穀梁所云『在祖位』者，謂孔子本宋孔父之後，防叔奔魯，遂爲魯人。
sentences.literal: What the Guliang calls "in the ancestor's place" means Confucius was originally descended from Song's Kong Fu; Fangshu fled to Lu and became a Lu man.
sentences.idiomatic: The "ancestor seat" means Confucius sprang from Song's Kong clan before Fangshu fled to Lu.

sentences.chinese: 今宋所殺者亦孔父之後留在宋者，於孔子爲祖列，故尊而不名也。」
sentences.literal: Now the man Song killed was also Kong Fu's descendant who remained in Song—in the ancestor column to Confucius, hence honored without naming."〉
sentences.idiomatic: The victim was another Kong kinsman left in Song—Confucius's senior line—hence the text withholds his name."

sentences.chinese: 〉此言孔子故殷後也，雖不正統，封其子孫以爲殷後，禮亦宜之。
sentences.literal: 〉This speech means Confucius was indeed Yin's descendant; though not the direct succession, to enfeoff his posterity as Yin's heir is also ritually proper.
sentences.idiomatic: The classic hints Confucius carried Yin blood; even off the main line, enfeoffing his seed as Yin heir fits the rites.

sentences.chinese: 何者？
sentences.literal: Why?
sentences.idiomatic: Why?

sentences.chinese: 諸侯奪宗，聖庶奪適。
sentences.literal: Feudal lords displace the main lineage; sage-born younger sons displace the heir.
sentences.idiomatic: Marquises can supplant the main house; wise younger sons can supplant the heir.

sentences.chinese: 〈如淳曰：「奪宗，始封之君尊爲諸侯，則奪其舊爲宗子之事也。
sentences.literal: 〈Ru Chun's note: "Duo zong means the first enfeoffed lord, honored as marquis, thereby strips the old duty of being lineage heir.
sentences.idiomatic: Ru Chun: a new marquis may displace the old zongzi role.

sentences.chinese: 奪適，文王舍伯邑考而立武王是也。
sentences.literal: Duo shi means King Wen passed over Bo Yikao and established King Wu."
sentences.idiomatic: Duo shi is Wen passing Bo Yikao for Wu."

sentences.chinese: 孔子雖庶，可爲殷後。」
sentences.literal: Confucius, though born a younger son, may serve as Yin's heir."〉
sentences.idiomatic: Confucius, though a cadet branch, may stand as Yin heir."

sentences.chinese: 師古曰：「適讀曰嫡。」
sentences.literal: Yan's note: "Shi is read di."〉
sentences.idiomatic: Yan Shigu: shi reads di, heir.

sentences.chinese: 〉傳曰「賢者子孫宜有土」，而況聖人，又殷之後哉！
sentences.literal: 〉The tradition says "the worthy man's descendants deserve land"—how much the more a sage who is also Yin's kin!
sentences.idiomatic: The canon says worthy lines deserve fiefs—how much more a sage of Yin stock!

sentences.chinese: 昔成王以諸侯禮葬周公，而皇天動威，雷風著災。
sentences.literal: Formerly King Cheng buried the Duke of Zhou with a feudal lord's rites, and High Heaven stirred its might—thunder and wind showed disaster.
sentences.idiomatic: When Cheng buried the Duke of Zhou with only a lord's honors, Heaven answered with storm and wind.

sentences.chinese: 〈師古曰：「尚書大傳云：『周公疾，曰：「吾死必葬於成周，示天下臣於成王也。」
sentences.literal: 〈Yan's note: "The Shang shu da zhuan says: when the Duke of Zhou fell ill he said, 'When I die bury me at Chengzhou to show the world I am subject to King Cheng.'"
sentences.idiomatic: Yan Shigu quotes the Great Tradition: Zhou asked burial at Chengzhou to show he served Cheng.

sentences.chinese: 周公死，天乃雷雨以風，禾盡偃，大木斯拔。
sentences.literal: When the Duke of Zhou died, Heaven then sent thunder, rain, and wind—the grain all lay flat, great trees were torn up.
sentences.idiomatic: At his death Heaven blasted wind and rain until grain flattened and trees uprooted.

sentences.chinese: 國恐，王與大夫開金縢之書，執書以泣曰：「周公勤勞王家，予幼人弗及知。」
sentences.literal: The state was afraid; the king with the great officers opened the metal-bound casket, took the document and wept, saying, "The Duke of Zhou was diligent for the royal house—I, a child, did not know it in time."
sentences.idiomatic: Panic gripped the court; king and ministers opened the golden coffer and wept that they had missed Zhou's loyalty.

sentences.chinese: 乃不葬於成周而葬之於畢，示天下不敢臣。』」
sentences.literal: Then he was not buried at Chengzhou but at Bi, to show the world they dared not treat him as subject."'"
sentences.idiomatic: They moved the tomb to Bi to show they dared not treat him as a vassal."

sentences.chinese: 〉今仲尼之廟不出闕里，〈師古曰：「闕里，孔子舊里也。
sentences.literal: 〉Now Confucius's temple does not step beyond Que village—〈Yan's note: "Que village is Confucius's old home."
sentences.idiomatic: Today Kong's shrine never leaves Queli—Yan Shigu names his lane.

sentences.chinese: 言除此之外，更無祭祀孔子者也。」
sentences.literal: It means beyond that there is no other place sacrificing to Confucius."〉
sentences.idiomatic: No second site honors the sage."

sentences.chinese: 〉孔氏子孫不免編戶，〈師古曰：「列爲庶人也。」
sentences.literal: 〉Kong descendants still rank as registered households—〈Yan's note: "They are listed as commoners."〉
sentences.idiomatic: His kin still pay poll tax as commoners—Yan Shigu notes their mean status.

sentences.chinese: 〉以聖人而歆匹夫之祀，非皇天之意也。
sentences.literal: 〉yet the sage receives only a commoner's offerings—this is not High Heaven's intent.
sentences.idiomatic: A sage fed like a peasant cannot be Heaven's will.

sentences.chinese: 今陛下誠能據仲尼之素功，以封其子孫，〈師古曰：「素功，素王之功也。
sentences.literal: Now if Your Majesty will truly take Confucius's uncrowned merit and enfeoff his descendants—〈Yan's note: "Su gong means the merit of the uncrowned king."
sentences.idiomatic: If you reward Confucius's "uncrowned king" merit with a fief—Yan Shigu cites the Guliang title.

sentences.chinese: 穀梁傳曰『孔子素王』。」
sentences.literal: The Guliang says "Confucius was the uncrowned king."'"
sentences.idiomatic: Guliang calls him the king without throne."

sentences.chinese: 〉則國家必獲其福，又陛下之名與天亡極。
sentences.literal: 〉then the state will surely receive its blessing, and Your Majesty's name will match Heaven without limit.
sentences.idiomatic: The dynasty will gain blessing and your name will ride the sky.

sentences.chinese: 何者？
sentences.literal: Why?
sentences.idiomatic: Why?

sentences.chinese: 追聖人素功，封其子孫，未有法也，後聖必以爲則。
sentences.literal: To pursue a sage's uncrowned merit and enfeoff his posterity sets no precedent—later sages will surely take it as model.
sentences.idiomatic: Honoring a throneless sage sets precedent for every age to come.

sentences.chinese: 不滅之名，可不勉哉！
sentences.literal: A name that is not extinguished—can you not strive for it!
sentences.idiomatic: Immortal fame is worth the reach.

sentences.chinese: 福孤遠，又譏切王氏，故終不見納。
sentences.literal: Fu was isolated and far, again cutting against the Wang clan, and therefore in the end was never accepted.
sentences.idiomatic: Mei Fu stood alone, slashed at the Wangs, and was never heard.

sentences.chinese: 至元始中，王莽顓政，〈師古曰：「顓讀與專同。」
sentences.literal: By the Yanshi era Wang Mang monopolized government—〈Yan's note: "Zhuan is read the same as zhuan."〉
sentences.idiomatic: In Yanshi Wang Mang seized the government—Yan Shigu reads zhuan as monopolize.

sentences.chinese: 〉福一朝棄妻子，去九江，至今傳以爲仙。
sentences.literal: 〉Fu in one morning abandoned wife and children, left Jiujiang, and to this day is handed down as an immortal.
sentences.idiomatic: He abandoned kin overnight, fled to Jiujiang, and legend makes him a transcendent.

sentences.chinese: 其後，人有見福於會稽者，變名姓，爲吳市門卒云。
sentences.literal: Afterward someone saw Fu in Kuaiji; he had changed his name and served as gate guard of Wu market, it is said.
sentences.idiomatic: Later rumor placed him in Kuaiji under a new name, tending a market gate.

sentences.chinese: 〈師古曰：「其後謂棄妻子去之後。」〉
sentences.literal: 〈Yan's note: "Afterward means after he abandoned wife and children and left."〉
sentences.idiomatic: Yan Shigu: afterward means after his flight.

sentences.chinese: 云敞字幼孺，平陵人也。
sentences.literal: Yun Chang, courtesy name Youru, was a man of Pingling.
sentences.idiomatic: Yun Chang, courtesy Youru, came from Pingling.

sentences.chinese: 師事同縣吳章，章治尚書經爲博士。
sentences.literal: He took as teacher Wu Zhang of the same county; Zhang studied the Documents classic and became an erudite.
sentences.idiomatic: He studied under Wu Zhang of his county; Zhang mastered the Shang shu and rose to erudite.

sentences.chinese: 平帝以中山王即帝位，年幼，莽秉政，自號安漢公。
sentences.literal: When Emperor Ping from Zhongshan took the throne, being young, Mang held power and styled himself Duke Who Pacifies Han.
sentences.idiomatic: Boy emperor Ping of Zhongshan left Mang as regent calling himself the Han-pacifying duke.

sentences.chinese: 以平帝爲成帝後，不得顧私親，帝母及外家衞氏皆留中山，不得至京師。
sentences.literal: Taking Ping as heir to Cheng, he could not heed private kin; the emperor's mother and the Wei maternal kin all remained in Zhongshan and could not come to the capital.
sentences.idiomatic: As Cheng's heir he barred his mother and the Wei in-laws from the capital.

sentences.chinese: 莽長子宇，非莽鬲絕衞氏，〈師古曰：「鬲讀與隔同。」
sentences.literal: Mang's eldest son Yu resented Mang for blocking the Wei—〈Yan's note: "Ge is read the same as ge."〉
sentences.idiomatic: Mang's heir Yu hated isolating the Wei clan—Yan Shigu reads ge as block.

sentences.chinese: 〉恐帝長大後見怨。
sentences.literal: 〉fearing that when the emperor grew he would harbor resentment.
sentences.idiomatic: He feared the boy emperor's future grudge.

sentences.chinese: 宇與吳章謀，夜以血塗莽門，若鬼神之戒，兾以懼莽。
sentences.literal: Yu plotted with Wu Zhang, smearing blood on Mang's gate by night as if spirits warned, hoping thereby to frighten Mang.
sentences.idiomatic: Yu and Zhang smeared Mang's gate with blood like a ghost warning.

sentences.chinese: 章欲因對其咎。
sentences.literal: Zhang wished thereby to get a chance to speak his blame.
sentences.idiomatic: Zhang meant to use the scare to open a remonstrance.

sentences.chinese: 事發覺，莽殺宇，誅滅衞氏，謀所聯及，死者百餘人。
sentences.literal: When the affair was discovered, Mang killed Yu, exterminated the Wei, and those implicated in the plot—more than a hundred died.
sentences.idiomatic: The plot surfaced; Mang killed Yu, wiped the Wei, and executed over a hundred linked names.

sentences.chinese: 章坐要斬，磔尸東市門。
sentences.literal: Zhang sat guilty of waist-cutting; his corpse was exposed at the eastern market gate.
sentences.idiomatic: Zhang died by waist-slicing; his body was displayed at the east market.

sentences.chinese: 初，章爲當世名儒，敎授尤盛，弟子千餘人，莽以爲惡人黨，皆當禁錮，不得仕宦。
sentences.literal: At first Zhang was a famous scholar of the age; his teaching was especially flourishing—more than a thousand disciples—and Mang deemed his faction evil; all were to be barred and forbidden office.
sentences.idiomatic: Zhang had been a celebrated teacher with a thousand pupils; Mang branded the school criminal and barred every man from office.

sentences.chinese: 門人盡更名他師。
sentences.literal: The disciples all changed names and took other teachers.
sentences.idiomatic: Students renounced him overnight and claimed new masters.

sentences.chinese: 〈師古曰：「更以他人爲師，諱不言是章弟子。」
sentences.literal: 〈Yan's note: "They took someone else as teacher, ashamed to say they were Zhang's disciples."〉
sentences.idiomatic: Yan Shigu: they hid ever having studied under Zhang.

sentences.chinese: 〉敞時爲大司徒掾，自劾吳章第子，收抱章尸歸，棺斂葬之，〈師古曰：「棺音工喚反。
sentences.literal: At that time Chang was a clerk in the Grand Minister of Education's bureau; he impeached himself as Zhang's disciple, collected and embraced Zhang's corpse, returned home, coffined and buried him—〈Yan's note: "Guan is read gong-huan fan.
sentences.idiomatic: Chang served as a clerk under the Grand Minister of Education; he confessed to being Zhang's pupil, reclaimed the corpse, and buried it—Yan Shigu gives fanqie for guan.

sentences.chinese: 斂音力贍反。」
sentences.literal: Lian is read li-shan fan."〉
sentences.idiomatic: Lian is read li-shan."

sentences.chinese: 〉京師稱焉。
sentences.literal: 〉The capital praised him.
sentences.idiomatic: Chang'an praised his courage.
